question: Define a compact metric space and state the Heine-Borel Theorem for the real numbers.
expectedPoints: A metric space (X, d) is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover.
The Heine-Borel Theorem states that a subset of R^n is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.
Specifically for R, a set is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.

question: Prove that a continuous image of a compact set is compact.
expectedPoints: Let f: X -> Y be a continuous function between metric spaces, and let K be a compact subset of X.
Consider an open cover {U_α} of f(K) in Y.
Since f is continuous, the preimages f^{-1}(U_α) form an open cover of K in X.
By the compactness of K, there exists a finite subcover f^{-1}(U_1), ..., f^{-1}(U_n).
Therefore, the corresponding sets U_1, ..., U_n form a finite subcover of f(K), proving it is compact.

question: Prove that every compact metric space is complete and totally bounded. Then, explain why the converse is also true (i.e., a metric space is compact if and only if it is complete and totally bounded).
expectedPoints: Proof of 'compact implies complete': A compact space is sequentially compact; any Cauchy sequence has a convergent subsequence, and thus the entire sequence converges.
Proof of 'compact implies totally bounded': For any ε > 0, the open balls {B(x, ε) : x ∈ X} form an open cover. A finite subcover exists, whose centers form a finite ε-net.

Explanation of the converse: Assume X is complete and totally bounded. To show it is compact, one can prove it is sequentially compact: any sequence has a Cauchy subsequence (due to total boundedness) which converges (due to completeness).
Since sequential compactness is equivalent to compactness in metric spaces, this completes the proof that X is compact.
